questions.text: The SI unit of electric charge is:
questions.explanation: Coulomb (C) is the SI unit of charge. One coulomb is equivalent to the charge contained in nearly 6 x 10¹⁸ electrons.

questions.text: The rate of flow of electric charges is known as:
questions.explanation: Electric current is defined as the amount of charge flowing through a particular area in unit time (I = Q/t).

questions.text: An instrument used to measure electric current in a circuit is called:
questions.explanation: An ammeter measures electric current and is always connected in series in a circuit.

questions.text: The work done to move a unit charge from one point to another is called:
questions.explanation: Potential difference (V) between two points is V = W/Q, where W is work done and Q is charge.

questions.text: One volt is equivalent to:
questions.explanation: 1 Volt is the potential difference when 1 Joule of work is done to move a charge of 1 Coulomb.

questions.text: According to Ohm's law, if the temperature remains constant, the current (I) is:
questions.explanation: Ohm's law states that V ∝ I, or V = IR, where R is a constant called resistance.

questions.text: The SI unit of resistance is:
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω). 1 Ω = 1V / 1A.

questions.text: Which of the following factors does NOT affect the resistance of a conductor?
questions.explanation: Resistance depends on length, area of cross-section, and the nature of the material (resistivity).

questions.text: The resistance of a conductor is ________ to its length.
questions.explanation: As the length of a wire increases, the resistance increases linearly (R ∝ l).

questions.text: The resistance of a conductor is ________ to its area of cross-section.
questions.explanation: A thicker wire has less resistance than a thinner wire of the same material and length (R ∝ 1/A).

questions.text: The SI unit of resistivity is:
questions.explanation: Resistivity (ρ) is a characteristic property of the material. Its unit is Ωm.

questions.text: Alloys are commonly used in electrical heating devices because:
options: They do not oxidise readily at high temperatures
questions.explanation: Alloys like Nichrome have higher resistivity than pure metals and do not burn (oxidise) even at high temperatures.

questions.text: When resistors are connected in series, the ________ remains the same through each resistor.
questions.explanation: In a series circuit, there is only one path for the flow of charge, so the current is identical in all parts.

questions.text: In a parallel circuit, the ________ is the same across all resistors.
questions.explanation: All components in a parallel circuit are connected across the same two points, ensuring the same voltage.

questions.text: The equivalent resistance of two resistors R₁ and R₂ connected in parallel is:
questions.explanation: 1/Rp = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂. Solving for Rp gives (R₁R₂) / (R₁ + R₂).

questions.text: Which of the following represents Joule's Law of Heating?
questions.explanation: All these formulas calculate the heat produced, depending on which variables (V, I, R) are known.

questions.text: The filament of an electric bulb is made of tungsten because:
options: It has a low melting point
It has a very high melting point
questions.explanation: Tungsten has a melting point of 3380°C, allowing it to remain solid while glowing white-hot.

questions.text: An electric fuse is used to protect circuits from:
questions.explanation: A fuse melts when excessive current flows, breaking the circuit and preventing damage.

questions.text: The commercial unit of electrical energy is:
questions.explanation: 1 kWh is the energy consumed by a 1 kW appliance in one hour, often called a 'unit'.

questions.text: 1 kWh is equal to how many Joules?
Electric power (P) can be expressed as:
questions.explanation: These are different forms of the power equation derived from P = VI and V = IR.

questions.text: If the current through a resistor is doubled (keeping R constant), the heat produced becomes:
questions.explanation: According to H ∝ I², doubling the current (2²) leads to four times the heat.

questions.text: A voltmeter is always connected in ________ with the circuit element.
questions.explanation: A voltmeter has high resistance and must be connected in parallel to measure potential difference without drawing significant current.

questions.text: What happens to the total resistance when more resistors are added in parallel?
questions.explanation: Adding more paths for current in parallel reduces the overall effective resistance.

questions.text: Which material is the best conductor of electricity?
questions.explanation: Silver has the lowest resistivity, but copper and aluminum are preferred for wiring due to cost.

questions.text: A component used to regulate current without changing the voltage source is:
questions.explanation: A rheostat (variable resistance) is used to control the amount of current in a circuit.

questions.text: The resistance of an ideal ammeter should be:
questions.explanation: An ideal ammeter should have zero resistance so it doesn't change the current it is measuring.

questions.text: The resistance of an ideal voltmeter should be:
questions.explanation: An ideal voltmeter should have infinite resistance so it draws no current from the circuit.

questions.text: If a wire is stretched to double its length, its resistance will:
questions.explanation: When stretched, length (l) doubles and area (A) becomes half (to keep volume constant). Since R ∝ l/A, R becomes 2 / (1/2) = 4 times.

questions.text: In domestic circuits, appliances are connected in parallel to:
options: Ensure each gets the same current
Ensure each gets the same voltage
questions.explanation: Parallel connection allows each appliance to operate at the same line voltage and work independently.

questions.text: The graph between potential difference (V) and current (I) for an ohmic conductor is a:
questions.explanation: Since V = IR, V is directly proportional to I, resulting in a linear graph.

questions.text: The slope of the V-I graph represents:
questions.explanation: Slope = ΔV / ΔI. From Ohm's law, this equals the Resistance (R).

questions.text: An electric bulb is rated 220V and 100W. When it is operated on 110V, the power consumed will be:
questions.explanation: Resistance R = V²/P = 220²/100 = 484Ω. New Power = V'²/R = 110²/484 = 12100/484 = 25W.

questions.text: Calculate the current if 50 Coulombs of charge flows through a wire in 10 seconds.
Potential difference is also known as:
questions.explanation: Voltage is the common term for electrical potential difference.

questions.text: If three 6Ω resistors are connected in parallel, the total resistance is:
Which of the following does NOT represent electrical power?
questions.explanation: IR² is not a formula for power; the correct term is I²R.

questions.text: The resistivity of a material depends on:
questions.explanation: Resistivity is an intrinsic property; it changes with the material type and temperature but not with dimensions.

questions.text: Current flows in a circuit only if the circuit is:
questions.explanation: A closed circuit provides a continuous path for the electrons to flow.

questions.text: What happens to the resistance of a semiconductor like silicon as temperature increases?
questions.explanation: Unlike metals, the resistance of semiconductors decreases with an increase in temperature.
